summary: A co-authored epidemiological study abstract examining the relationship between light-to-moderate alcohol consumption and mortality in men. The findings support a U-shaped relationship, where light-to-moderate drinking reduces overall mortality risk, primarily driven by a decrease in cardiovascular disease mortality.

principles.statement: Light-to-moderate alcohol consumption exhibits a U-shaped relationship with total mortality in men.
principles.rationale: Light-to-moderate drinking decreases cardiovascular disease mortality without significantly increasing the risk of common site-specific cancers, leading to an overall reduction in mortality risk at those levels.
principles.source_quote: These data support a U-shaped relation between alcohol and total mortality among light-to-moderate drinking men.

mental_models.name: U-shaped risk curve
mental_models.description: An epidemiological model where risk (such as mortality) is higher at zero exposure, dips to its lowest point at low-to-moderate exposure, and rises again at high exposure.
mental_models.example: Applied to alcohol consumption, where abstainers have higher mortality than light-to-moderate drinkers, but heavy drinkers have the highest mortality.
